fabricated_divergences.quote: Spanish-language sources from both Mexico (El Financiero) and Argentina (Infobae) report detailed medical and emergency response data — including the 1,615 people treated, specific injury types (fractures, cuts, intoxication, anxiety), and the reactivation of the Ley Seca alcohol ban — whereas English-language sources largely omit these operational details.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The analysis of Article 0 (Anadolu, an English-language outlet) explicitly states '1,615 received medical attention,' so the 1,615-treated figure is not Spanish-exclusive as implied. The injury-type detail and Ley Seca reactivation are indeed Spanish-only, so the contrast partially holds, but citing the 1,615 figure as a Spanish-vs-English differentiator is contradicted by the ground truth.

fabricated_divergences.quote: The separate vehicle-strike incident in Baja California / Cabo San Lucas, in which a driver was killed by fans after his car struck a supporter, is reported by Turkish (Anadolu) and Argentine/Mexican Spanish sources (El Financiero, Infobae) but is absent from the UK and German English coverage (BBC, DW, Guardian).
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: Per the analyses, the Baja California/Cabo San Lucas incident appears only in Article 0 (Anadolu, Turkey) and Article 3 (El Financiero, Mexico). Neither Infobae article (5 or 7) mentions this incident in its summary, so attributing it to Argentine Infobae is unsupported.

fabricated_divergences.quote: DW News (German, English) reports only three suffocation deaths and frames the event as a crowd crush, while all other language and regional sources report four deaths including a fourth from cardiorespiratory arrest linked to an epileptic seizure.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The claim that 'all other language and regional sources report four deaths' is contradicted by Article 6 (El Financiero, Mexico), whose analysis states 'three deaths from asphyxiation.' DW is not unique in reporting three deaths, and this is a single-article count difference rather than a genuine cross-group framing contrast.

fabricated_divergences.quote: Spanish-language sources embed the celebration deaths within Mexico's broader security context — including drug trafficking, organized crime, and the UK government's updated travel advisory — while English-language sources treat the deaths as an isolated crowd-safety incident without connecting them to wider security concerns.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: The drug-trafficking/organized-crime roundup and the UK travel advisory appear only in Article 7 (Infobae, Argentina). The Mexican Spanish sources (El Financiero, Articles 3, 6, 8) do not embed the deaths within a broader security/organized-crime context; they focus on governance and administrative response. Attributing this framing to 'Spanish-language sources' generally overstates a single Argentine outlet's approach as a group-wide pattern.

fabricated_divergences.quote: Western English-language outlets (BBC, DW News, The Guardian) omit the separate driver-related death in Baja California/Cabo San Lucas, while the Turkish outlet Anadolu Agency and the Mexican/Argentine Spanish-language outlets (El Financiero, Infobae) include this second fatal incident.
fabricated_divergences.why_unsupported: Per the analyses, only Article 0 (Anadolu) and Article 3 (El Financiero) report the Baja California/Cabo San Lucas driver incident. Neither Infobae article (5 or 7) mentions it, so including Argentine Infobae among outlets covering this incident is unsupported by the ground truth.
